summary: That morning, I first heard that a plane had hit the World Trade Center, though at first we thought it might have been an accident. Then a second plane hit the other tower, and I realized this was no accident but a terrorist act. This was the start of the September 11 attacks, and it set everything in motion for us that day.

I was in Hancock, Maryland that morning when I got the news about the planes hitting the World Trade Center. I went to the local Ranger station there to meet a fellow Ranger and we started gathering information from our Communications Center. Hancock was the starting point for us before heading toward the National Capital Region.
cues: starting point before heading to DC

summary: Our Communications Center serves the entire National Capital Region, including DC and surrounding areas. On September 11, it was the hub for receiving all the incoming information about the attacks, including reports about the Pentagon and other incidents. It was physically located here at Ferry Hill, and we relied on it heavily for coordination.

There was a report early on that there had been a bombing on the Mall, but that turned out to be false. Later, a second Park Service team was sent to the Mall to assist with security and coordination. The Park Police secured the Mall area along with our team while we were handling other assignments.

We learned that a plane had struck the Pentagon, and that was a critical part of the day's events. The daycare center from the Pentagon was evacuated to the Clara Barton Parkway, where we secured and cared for the children and staff. Traveling from the daycare to the DOT building took us right past the front of the Pentagon, where the damage was visible and emotional for some.

During the chaos, we heard that another plane was missing and feared down in Pennsylvania. This was later confirmed as part of the September 11 attacks. Although we were not directly involved there, it was part of the overall situation we were responding to.

Ferry Hill was where our National Capital Region Communications Center is located, and it served as a staging and coordination point that morning. I was there after leaving Hancock, and we gathered supplies and prepared to head into DC from that location. It was a key site for communication and organizing our response.

The staging area for our deployment into DC was at the George Washington Memorial Parkway headquarters. From there, we received our assignments and were dispatched to various locations, including the Clara Barton Parkway. It was a central point for organizing law enforcement efforts that day.

We were sent to the Clara Barton Parkway to secure the area where the Pentagon daycare children and staff had been evacuated. They were gathered on the grass adjacent to the westbound lane next to the Potomac River. We set up a perimeter, provided supplies like diapers and blankets, and later transported them to the DOT building for safety.

The Potomac River runs alongside the Clara Barton Parkway where we secured the Pentagon daycare children and staff on the grassy area. It was a natural boundary for the perimeter we set up to keep the group safe. The proximity to the river was notable as we managed traffic and security in that area.

An Air Force Captain was present at the Clara Barton Parkway site overseeing the evacuation of the Pentagon daycare children. He coordinated acquiring the use of the DOT facility to move the children to a safer indoor location. I also transported him and his wife home later since they couldn't access their vehicles due to road closures.

The DOT building, located next to the Naval Annex, was used as a safe shelter for the Pentagon daycare children and staff after we evacuated them from the Clara Barton Parkway. The Air Force Captain helped secure this facility for us. We set up security there, provided food and supplies, and managed traffic as it became a staging point for various agencies.

The Naval Annex is located near the DOT building where we relocated the Pentagon daycare children and staff. It served as a geographic reference point for the shelter site. The proximity allowed us to coordinate security and support efficiently during the emergency response.

The FBI was present at the DOT building staging area along with State Police and other agencies. We assisted with traffic control to facilitate their movement and operations. They were part of the multi-agency response to the attacks that day.

The United States Park Police worked alongside our Park Service teams to secure the Mall and other key areas in DC during the September 11 attacks. One of our teams was assigned to assist them at the Mall, helping to maintain order and safety. They were an important partner in the law enforcement response.

The September 11 attacks unfolded rapidly that morning, starting with the planes hitting the World Trade Center, then the Pentagon, and the crash in Pennsylvania. I was part of the response, coordinating Rangers and securing evacuees, especially the Pentagon daycare children. It was a day of chaos, urgency, and cooperation among many agencies to protect and assist those affected.
cues: World Trade Center and Pentagon attacks
